title: How long does it take to charge an electric car?
description: Real UK charging times for electric cars: 3-pin plug, 7kW home wallbox, 50kW rapid and 150kW ultra-rapid, plus what each charge costs in 2026.
blurb: From 30 minutes at a rapid charger to a full day on a 3-pin plug. The real UK numbers.
lede: Ask how long an electric car takes to charge and you will get answers ranging from twenty minutes to more than a day, and the frustrating thing is that all of them are true. Charging time depends on the size of the battery, the power of the charger, how full the battery already is, and even the weather. The trick is to stop thinking of it like filling a petrol tank, where you stand next to the pump until it clicks. Most EV drivers charge overnight at home while they sleep, so the honest answer for day-to-day life is that charging takes about ten seconds: the time it takes to plug in. This guide gives you the real numbers for every charger type, a simple formula for your own car, and what each option costs.
short_answer: A typical 60kWh electric car takes around 6 to 7 hours to charge from 10% to 80% on a 7kW home wallbox, which fits comfortably overnight. A 50kW public rapid charger does the same in roughly an hour, and a 150kW ultra-rapid charger in about 30 minutes if the car can accept that speed. A standard 3-pin plug is the slow road: expect 18 hours or more for the same charge.

sections.html:
Five things set how long a charge takes, and only two of them are under your control on the day.

  • Charger power. Measured in kilowatts (kW). A home wallbox delivers 7kW, public rapid chargers start at 50kW, and ultra-rapid units run from 100kW to 350kW.
  • The car's maximum charging rate. This is the ceiling. Plug a car that accepts a maximum of 50kW into a 350kW charger and you still get 50kW. Check your car's spec for both its AC limit (home and lamp-post charging, usually 7kW or 11kW) and its DC limit (rapid charging).
  • Battery size. A 40kWh battery fills faster than an 80kWh one at the same power, in the same way a kettle fills faster than a bathtub from the same tap.
  • State of charge. Charging is quick in the middle of the battery and slow at the top. Almost every car charges fastest between about 10% and 80%, then tapers sharply to protect the cells. The last 20% can take as long as the previous 60%.
  • Temperature. A cold battery accepts less power. Rapid charging in winter can take noticeably longer unless the car warms the battery on the way to the charger. There is more on this in our guide to EV charging in winter.

The rough formula is simple: charging time in hours = energy needed in kWh divided by charger power in kW. Add around 10 to 15% on top for home charging, because some energy is lost as heat in the onboard charger and cable.

The table below shows approximate times to charge from 10% to 80%, the band where charging runs at full speed and the way most drivers actually use public chargers. Times assume the car can accept the charger's full power; many smaller EVs cap rapid charging at 50 to 100kW, so the ultra-rapid row only applies to cars built for it.

Charger typePower40kWh battery60kWh battery80kWh battery
3-pin plug (granny cable)2.3kW12 to 14 hrs18 to 20 hrs24+ hrs
Home wallbox7kW4 to 4.5 hrs6 to 7 hrs8 to 9 hrs
Public fast (AC)7 to 22kW1.5 to 4.5 hrs2 to 7 hrs3 to 9 hrs
Public rapid (DC)50kW35 to 45 min50 to 65 min70 to 85 min
Ultra-rapid (DC)150kW+25 to 35 min25 to 35 min30 to 40 min

Approximate 10% to 80% times including typical charging losses and taper. Actual speed depends on the car's maximum charging rate and battery temperature.

Two things jump out. First, the 22kW row is misleading for most cars: the majority of EVs sold in the UK only accept 7kW or 11kW on AC, so a 22kW post often charges no faster than your wallbox at home. Second, the ultra-rapid times for the 40kWh and 60kWh cars are similar because smaller batteries usually have lower maximum charging rates, so the charger spends part of the session waiting for the car.

sections.h2: Home charging: slow is fine when you are asleep
sections.html:
Around 80% of EV charging in the UK happens at home, and the 6 to 7 hours a wallbox needs for a decent charge sounds long until you remember your car sits on the drive for 12 hours most nights. A 7kW wallbox adds roughly 25 to 30 miles of range per hour, so even a nearly flat big battery is full by morning. For typical UK mileage of around 20 to 25 miles a day, you only need to plug in two or three nights a week.

Overnight charging is also where the money is. Off-peak EV tariffs charge 7 to 9p per kWh during a fixed night window, against 26.11p on the standard price cap, and the cheapest schemes run closer to 5.5p. Set the car or wallbox to charge inside that window and a full charge costs a quarter of the daytime price. Our guides to what home EV charging costs and the best time to charge an electric car cover the schedules in detail.

What about the 3-pin plug?

The granny cable that comes with most cars draws about 2.3kW from a normal socket, adding only 8 to 10 miles of range per hour. That is workable as a backup, or if you drive very few miles, but a full charge on a 60kWh car takes the best part of a day and a standard socket is not designed for that sustained load. If you have off-street parking, a dedicated wallbox is safer, three times faster, and the only practical way to use a cheap overnight window properly. We compare the two in home EV charger vs 3-pin plug.

Rapid and ultra-rapid chargers are for journeys, not routine. On a motorway trip the rhythm is simple: arrive with 10 to 20% in the battery, charge to 80%, and leave. That middle band is where the car accepts its maximum rate, so a modern long-range EV adds 150 to 200 miles in 25 to 35 minutes on an ultra-rapid unit, which is roughly the length of a coffee and a toilet stop.

Charging past 80% at a rapid charger is usually a mistake. The car deliberately slows down to protect the battery, so the final 20% can take another half hour while you pay rapid prices, around 79p per kWh in mid 2026, for electricity arriving at wallbox speed. Many networks also add overstay fees. Unless the next charger is beyond your 80% range, drive on and charge again sooner.

A few practical points for trips:

  • Use the car's navigation to precondition the battery before a rapid stop. A warm battery can halve winter charging times.
  • Check the car's DC maximum before paying ultra-rapid prices. If the car tops out at 50kW, a cheaper 50kW unit charges it just as fast.
  • Speeds quoted on the charger are peaks, not averages. A "150kW" session on most cars averages a fair bit less across 10% to 80%.

Prices vary a lot by network and speed, and our guide to public EV charging costs breaks down where the money goes.

sections.h2: What each charge costs while you wait
sections.html:
Speed and price pull in opposite directions: the fastest electricity is the most expensive. Here is the same charge, 10% to 80% on a 60kWh car (about 42kWh delivered, 46kWh drawn from the supply at home after losses), priced at mid 2026 rates.

Where you chargeRateTimeCost
Home, EV off-peak tariff7p/kWh6 to 7 hrs overnight£3.22
Home, standard rate26.11p/kWh6 to 7 hrs£12.01
Public standard charger54p/kWh2 to 7 hrs£22.68
Public rapid charger79p/kWh30 to 65 min£33.18

Standard rate 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. Off-peak and public rates are typical mid 2026 figures (Zapmap averages for public charging); your tariff and network will differ.

The gap is striking: the same energy costs ten times more at a rapid charger than on a good overnight tariff. That is why the single biggest factor in your annual charging bill is not how long charging takes but where and when you do it. If you can shift most of your charging into a cheap night window, the slow option is also by far the cheapest. Our guide to EV electricity tariffs explains how the off-peak windows and rates work, and work out your EV charging cost walks through the sums for your own car and mileage.

How long an electric car takes to charge depends almost entirely on where you plug it in. On a 7kW home wallbox, a typical 60kWh car needs 6 to 7 hours for a 10% to 80% charge, which disappears into an overnight schedule and costs around £3 on an off-peak EV tariff. On a 50kW rapid charger the same charge takes about an hour; on a 150kW ultra-rapid unit, around half an hour if the car can take the power. The 3-pin plug works in a pinch but turns charging into an all-day affair.

For most drivers the practical answer is reassuringly dull: plug in two or three nights a week, let the car charge while you sleep, and save rapid chargers for long trips, charging to 80% and no further. Do that and charging time stops being a number you ever think about, while the cost per mile drops to a fraction of petrol.

faq.q: Can I charge an electric car from a normal 3-pin socket?
faq.a: Yes, with the granny cable supplied with most cars, but it only delivers about 2.3kW, which adds 8 to 10 miles of range per hour. A full charge on a 60kWh car takes the best part of a day. It is fine as a backup or for very low mileage, but a 7kW wallbox is around three times faster, safer for regular use, and works far better with cheap overnight tariffs.

faq.q: Why does charging slow down after 80%?
faq.a: As the battery fills, the car reduces charging power to protect the cells from stress and heat. The effect is small at home but dramatic on rapid chargers, where the final 20% can take as long as the previous 60%. On journeys it is usually quicker and cheaper to stop at 80% and charge again later if needed.

faq.q: Does cold weather make charging slower?
faq.a: Yes. A cold battery accepts less power, so rapid charging in winter can take significantly longer. Many EVs can precondition, meaning they warm the battery on the way to a rapid charger, which restores most of the lost speed. Home overnight charging is barely affected because the lower power and longer window absorb the difference.

faq.q: How often do I need to charge an electric car?
faq.a: For typical UK mileage of 20 to 25 miles a day and a modern 200-mile-plus car, plugging in two or three nights a week is plenty. Most drivers set a charge limit of around 80% for daily use and only charge to 100% before a long trip.

faq.q: Will a 22kW public charger charge my car three times faster than my wallbox?
faq.a: Usually not. Most EVs sold in the UK accept a maximum of 7kW or 11kW on AC charging, so a 22kW post delivers no more than the car's own limit. Only cars with a 22kW onboard charger, which are relatively rare, get the full speed. Check your car's AC charging spec before paying extra for a faster AC post.
